news_summary: Today's technology news highlights breakthroughs in biological research, advanced 3D micromanipulation tools, renewed international scientific collaboration, and innovative plans for space-based AI data centers.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité technologique d'aujourd'hui met en avant des avancées majeures dans la recherche biologique, des outils de micromanipulation 3D de pointe, un regain de collaboration scientifique internationale et des projets innovants de centres de données d'IA en orbite.
news_summary_es: Las noticias tecnológicas de hoy destacan los avances en la investigación biológica, las herramientas avanzadas de micromanipulación en 3D, la renovada colaboración científica internacional y los planes innovadores para centros de datos de IA situados en el espacio.

articles.title: Cell transplant across the tree of life hints at how animals emerged
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 18 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01910-0Embryonic ‘organizer cells’ can tell embryos of various phyla what kind of body to build.
articles.content:
Researchers transplanted cells between embryos of a comb jelly (Mnemiopsis leidyi, right) and a starlet sea anemone (Nematostella vectensis, left).Credit: Paul R. Sterry/Nature Photographers Ltd/Alamy, Phil Degginger/Science Photo Library

More than a century ago, embryologist Hilde Mangold conducted a strange experiment that transformed biology.

As a PhD student in the 1920s, she moved a lump of cells from embryos of one newt species into another. The transplanted cells caused a secondary ‘body axis’ to form in the host embryo, complete with a nervous system and a precursor to the spine. Mangold showed that much of the secondary body axis developed from the recipient embryo tissues.

The discovery of an embryonic ‘organizer’ that orchestrates the formation of a body axis “established a whole new area of developmental biology”, says Stanislav Kremnyov, a developmental biologist at the Friedrich Schiller University Jena in Germany, who is now following in Mangold’s footsteps.

In a study published in Nature on 17 June1, Kremnyov and his colleagues report the discovery of an embryonic organizer in marine predators called comb jellies (Ctenophora) and their successful transplantation into sea anemones (Cnidaria) — which belong to an entirely different phylum — forming extra mouths and pharynxes.

Many scientists think comb jellies belong to the earliest branch of the animal family tree. Kremnyov and his colleagues argue the appearance of an organizer helped transform single-celled organisms into animals.

“I’m really excited to see this,” says Ulrich Technau, an evolutionary developmental biologist at the University of Vienna. But not all scientists are convinced by the claims.

Building a second body

Mangold died tragically in 1924 in a home gas explosion, but the discovery garnered her supervisor, Hans Spemann, the 1935 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine.

Spemann’s laboratory became the centre of the developmental biology world, attracting leading scientists to probe the organizer and look for similar capabilities in other developing tissues.

Ancient sea jelly makes tree of life wobble

“They did insane things,” says Kremnyov, transplanting tissue from snails and mouse kidneys into frog embryos, showing that these, too, possessed organizational activity. Later, in the 1980s and 1990s, scientists uncovered the molecular basis for organizers: signalling molecules known as morphogens.

Kremnyov says that that organizer cells and the morphogens they release act like a construction foreman in the developing embryo, telling “the cells what they have to do and what they have to build”.

Research into the organizer has been focused mostly on vertebrates. But in 2007, a team led by Technau discovered such cells in the embryos of starlet sea anemones (Nematostella vectensis)2, which belong to an animal lineage that diverged after that of comb jellies. The findings raised questions about the evolutionary origins of the organizer.

“Until our work, it was not known when it emerged,” says Kremnyov.

Hilde Mangold, shown here holding her baby, discovered embryonic ‘organiser cells' in 1924 during her PhD.Credit: Science Source/Science Photo Library

Two mouths

Kremnyov’s supervisor and study co-author, developmental biologist Andreas Hejnol at the Friedrich Schiller University Jena, maintains a menagerie of unusual invertebrates — including sea anemones, penis worms and flatworms — that he calls a microzoo. When he joined the lab, Kremnyov added a comb jelly (Mnemiopsis leidyi).

What were the first animals? The fierce sponge–jelly battle that just won’t end

M. leidyi are highly fecund and lay transparent embryos daily. While examining M. leidyi, Kremnyov observed the formation of embryo structures called blastopores — the location of the embryonic organizer in other animals. “I saw the blastopore — and what I wanted to do was just transplant it.” The structures appear during a process called gastrulation, which occurs hours after fertilization in comb jellies.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in developmental biology, revealing the discovery and transplantation of embryonic organizer cells across distant animal phyla. This finding advances understanding of animal evolution and embryonic development, with broad implications for biology and related fields.
articles.personality_title: Scientists transplant organizer cells between distant marine animals, revealing clues to animal origins
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – More than 100 years ago, a scientist named Hilde Mangold discovered special cells in embryos that can organize how a body forms. These cells, called embryonic organizers, tell the developing embryo what parts to build. Until recently, scientists mostly studied these cells in animals like frogs and mice.

**What happened** – Researchers led by Stanislav Kremnyov successfully took organizer cells from embryos of comb jellies, simple marine animals, and transplanted them into sea anemone embryos. These two animals belong to very different groups, separated by hundreds of millions of years of evolution. The transplanted cells caused the sea anemone embryos to grow extra mouths and parts of the throat.

**Impact** – This is the first time embryonic organizer cells have been shown to work across such distant animal groups. It suggests that these organizing cells appeared very early in animal evolution and helped single-celled organisms become complex animals. The finding gives scientists new ideas about how animals first evolved and developed their body shapes.

**What's next step** – Scientists will study how these organizer cells work in other animals and learn more about the molecules they use to send signals. This research could help explain the detailed steps animals took to evolve from simple beginnings. It might also improve how we understand and treat developmental problems in animals and humans.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Transplanting organizer cells between very different marine animals shows these cells played a key role in the early evolution of animal bodies.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des cellules organisatrices transplantées entre animaux marins éloignés révèlent les origines des animaux
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Il y a plus de 100 ans, une scientifique nommée Hilde Mangold a découvert des cellules spéciales dans les embryons capables d’organiser la formation du corps. Ces cellules, appelées cellules organisatrices embryonnaires, indiquent à l’embryon ce qu’il doit construire. Jusqu’à récemment, les chercheurs étudiaient surtout ces cellules chez des animaux comme les grenouilles et les souris.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs dirigés par Stanislav Kremnyov ont réussi à transférer des cellules organisatrices provenant d’embryons de méduses peignes, des animaux marins simples, dans des embryons d’anémones de mer. Ces deux animaux appartiennent à des groupes très différents, séparés par des centaines de millions d’années d’évolution. Les cellules transplantées ont fait pousser des bouches et des parties de la gorge supplémentaires chez les embryons d’anémones.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois que des cellules organisatrices embryonnaires fonctionnent entre des groupes d’animaux aussi éloignés. Cela suggère que ces cellules sont apparues très tôt dans l’évolution des animaux et ont aidé des organismes unicellulaires à devenir des animaux complexes. Cette découverte offre de nouvelles idées sur comment les animaux ont évolué et formé leurs corps.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont étudier comment ces cellules organisatrices fonctionnent chez d’autres animaux et comprendre mieux les molécules qu’elles utilisent pour envoyer des signaux. Cette recherche pourrait expliquer les étapes précises que les animaux ont suivies pour évoluer à partir de formes simples. Elle pourrait aussi améliorer la compréhension et le traitement des problèmes de développement chez les animaux et les humains.

**En une phrase** – La transplantation de cellules organisatrices entre animaux marins très différents montre que ces cellules ont joué un rôle clé dans l’évolution précoce des corps animaux.
articles.personality_title_es: Células organizadoras trasplantadas entre animales marinos distantes revelan pistas sobre el origen de los animales
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Hace más de 100 años, una científica llamada Hilde Mangold descubrió células especiales en embriones que pueden organizar cómo se forma un cuerpo. Estas células, llamadas organizadoras embrionarias, indican al embrión qué partes debe construir. Hasta hace poco, los científicos estudiaban principalmente estas células en animales como ranas y ratones.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores liderados por Stanislav Kremnyov lograron tomar células organizadoras de embriones de medusas peine, animales marinos simples, y trasplantarlas a embriones de anémonas de mar. Estos dos animales pertenecen a grupos muy diferentes, separados por cientos de millones de años de evolución. Las células trasplantadas hicieron que los embriones de anémonas crecieran bocas y partes de la garganta adicionales.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que se demuestra que las células organizadoras embrionarias funcionan entre grupos animales tan distantes. Esto sugiere que estas células aparecieron muy temprano en la evolución animal y ayudaron a que organismos unicelulares se convirtieran en animales complejos. El hallazgo ofrece nuevas ideas sobre cómo evolucionaron y se formaron los cuerpos de los animales.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos estudiarán cómo funcionan estas células organizadoras en otros animales y aprenderán más sobre las moléculas que usan para enviar señales. Esta investigación podría explicar los pasos detallados que siguieron los animales para evolucionar desde formas simples. También podría mejorar la comprensión y el tratamiento de problemas de desarrollo en animales y humanos.

**Una frase clave** – Trasplantar células organizadoras entre animales marinos muy diferentes muestra que estas células jugaron un papel clave en la evolución temprana de los cuerpos animales.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of two translucent marine embryos—a comb jelly on the right and a starlet sea anemone on the left—connected by a glowing, delicate thread of cells being gently transplanted from one to the other, surrounded by soft, flowing water currents symbolizing early animal development and the birth of new body structures.

articles.title: Optical fibre gripper for high-performance 3D micromanipulation
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 17 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10673-7A miniature three-dimensional optical fibre gripper enables powerful, precise micromanipulation of particles and single cells in confined spaces, bridging the gap between optical and mechanical tweezers.
articles.content: Optical tweezers offer precise, non-contact control, but operate in a limited force regime and impose strict requirements on the characteristics of the targets as well as the environmental conditions1,2,3,4. Millimetre-scale mechanical tweezers can offer higher gripping force but are not suitable for precise manipulations5,6,7,8,9,10,11. Integrating microgrippers directly at the optical fibres provides a new approach for precise micromanipulation. However, existing fibre-integrated tweezers still face challenges in achieving high-performance manipulation of micro-objects (for example, single cells) within narrow spaces, mainly due to simplified architectures, constrained designs and millimetre-scale footprints12,13,14. Here we report a three-dimensional (3D) optical fibre gripper (OFG), which is fabricated by two-step, two-photon polymerization. The OFG consists of rigid photoresist microclaws and soft thermoresponsive hydrogel muscle doped with silver nanoparticles, and its size is only 38 × 38 × 61 μm3. The OFG exhibits a force-to-mass ratio of about 340 μN mg−1, outperforming previously reported fibre-integrated tweezers by one to two orders of magnitude. The OFG can manipulate opaque particles, irregular micromechanical components and diverse single-cell types. We further demonstrated its potential in 3D microassembly of complex microdevices (bearings, shafts and gearboxes) and biomimetic sampling in the narrow environment (<300 μm). These results position the OFG as a compact fibre-tip manipulator for 3D micromanipulation, offering reversible and tunable gripping in an intermediate force regime between optical field trapping and millimetre-scale mechanical tweezers.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant technological breakthrough in 3D micromanipulation with a novel optical fibre gripper that enables precise and powerful manipulation of micro-objects including single cells in confined spaces. This advancement bridges gaps between existing optical and mechanical tweezers, offering broad potential applications in microassembly and biomedical fields, thus having meaningful real-world impact and broad significance.
articles.personality_title: Tiny 3D optical fibre gripper advances precise control of micro-objects
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists use tools called optical and mechanical tweezers to move tiny things like cells or small parts. Optical tweezers can control objects without touching them but only work with light objects and specific conditions. Mechanical tweezers are stronger but too big for very precise work in small spaces.

**What happened** – Researchers created a very small 3D optical fibre gripper, just about 38 micrometers wide. It has tiny claws made with special light-sensitive materials and soft muscles that respond to heat. This gripper can hold and move small particles, irregular parts, and single cells even in tight spaces smaller than 300 micrometers. It was made using a high-precision printing method called two-photon polymerization.

**Impact** – This new gripper is much stronger and more precise than previous fibre-based tweezers, able to apply forces 10 to 100 times greater compared to older versions. It can handle a wider variety of tiny objects and work inside very narrow spaces. This makes it useful for building tiny machines and studying cells in ways not possible before.

**What's next step** – Scientists plan to explore using this gripper for assembling complex microdevices and for biomedical research, such as sampling cells in small environments. Its ability to grip gently and strongly could open new possibilities in medicine and engineering.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A tiny 3D optical fibre gripper now allows powerful and precise handling of microscopic objects, bridging the gap between delicate optical tweezers and strong mechanical tools.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un micro-preneur optique 3D miniature améliore le contrôle précis des micro-objets
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques utilisent des outils appelés pinces optiques et mécaniques pour déplacer de très petits objets comme des cellules ou des petites pièces. Les pinces optiques contrôlent sans contact mais ne fonctionnent que sur des objets légers et dans des conditions spécifiques. Les pinces mécaniques sont plus fortes mais trop grandes pour des travaux très précis dans des espaces réduits.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont créé un micro-preneur optique 3D très petit, d’environ 38 micromètres de large. Il possède de minuscules griffes fabriquées avec des matériaux sensibles à la lumière et des muscles souples qui réagissent à la chaleur. Cette pince peut saisir et déplacer de petites particules, des pièces irrégulières et des cellules individuelles, même dans des espaces étroits de moins de 300 micromètres. Elle a été fabriquée grâce à une méthode d’impression très précise appelée polymérisation biphotonique.

**Impact** – Cette nouvelle pince est beaucoup plus forte et précise que les pinces à fibres optiques précédentes, capable d’appliquer des forces 10 à 100 fois supérieures. Elle peut manipuler une plus grande variété d’objets minuscules et travailler dans des espaces très étroits. Cela la rend utile pour construire de minuscules machines et étudier les cellules de façons inédites.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques prévoient d’utiliser cette pince pour assembler des microdispositifs complexes et pour la recherche biomédicale, comme l’échantillonnage de cellules dans de petits environnements. Sa capacité à saisir avec douceur et force pourrait ouvrir de nouvelles possibilités en médecine et en ingénierie.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un micro-preneur optique 3D miniature permet désormais de manipuler avec puissance et précision des objets microscopiques, comblant le fossé entre les pinces optiques délicates et les outils mécaniques puissants.
articles.personality_title_es: Mini pinza óptica 3D mejora el control preciso de micro-objetos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos usan herramientas llamadas pinzas ópticas y mecánicas para mover cosas muy pequeñas como células o piezas diminutas. Las pinzas ópticas controlan sin tocar pero solo funcionan con objetos ligeros y en ciertas condiciones. Las pinzas mecánicas son más fuertes pero demasiado grandes para trabajos muy precisos en espacios pequeños.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores crearon una pinza óptica 3D muy pequeña, de solo unos 38 micrómetros de ancho. Tiene garras diminutas hechas con materiales sensibles a la luz y músculos suaves que reaccionan al calor. Esta pinza puede sostener y mover partículas pequeñas, piezas irregulares y células individuales incluso en espacios estrechos menores a 300 micrómetros. Fue fabricada usando una técnica de impresión muy precisa llamada polimerización por dos fotones.

**Impacto** – Esta nueva pinza es mucho más fuerte y precisa que las pinzas ópticas anteriores, capaz de aplicar fuerzas 10 a 100 veces mayores. Puede manipular una mayor variedad de objetos muy pequeños y trabajar en espacios muy reducidos. Esto la hace útil para construir máquinas diminutas y estudiar células de formas que antes no eran posibles.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos planean usar esta pinza para ensamblar microdispositivos complejos y para investigaciones biomédicas, como tomar muestras de células en ambientes pequeños. Su capacidad para agarrar con suavidad y fuerza podría abrir nuevas posibilidades en medicina e ingeniería.

**Resumen en una frase** – Una pequeña pinza óptica 3D permite ahora manipular objetos microscópicos con fuerza y precisión, uniendo la delicadeza de las pinzas ópticas con la fuerza de las mecánicas.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a delicate, miniature mechanical claw made of translucent materials gently grasping a tiny, irregularly shaped micro-object suspended in a softly glowing, narrow space, with subtle hints of silver nanoparticles shimmering within the claw’s flexible, muscle-like structure, evoking precise and careful 3D micromanipulation at a microscopic scale.

articles.title: Brexit tore apart European science — now the research rifts are healing
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 18 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01841-wThe UK’s share of EU research funding is climbing, but lost networks will be harder to recover.
articles.content:
UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer meets European Commission president Ursula von der Leyen. The United Kingdom and European Union will resume ‘reset talks’ in July.Credit: Benjamin Cremel/AFP/Getty

Ten years after the United Kingdom voted to leave the European Union, there are signs that their relationship in research is healing.

One big win came with an agreement in April: starting in 2027, the United Kingdom will rejoin Erasmus+, an EU exchange scheme used by PhD students and university staff. Another is the news that the UK share of funding from the EU’s flagship €95-billion (US$110-billion) Horizon Europe funding scheme has begun to recover after the country rejoined the programme in 2024.

UK participation in EU research projects had tanked in the wilderness years between Brexit and the country’s reassociation with Horizon Europe, but has increased since 2024 (see ‘EU funding access’).

The political will to further rebuild UK–EU science relations is there, in part. The United Kingdom’s Labour government is slowly following through with its manifesto pledge to build a closer relationship with the EU. As part of ‘reset’ talks that began at a summit in May last year, the EU and United Kingdom hope eventually to reach deals on science-related issues, such as a mobility programme enabling young people to study and work across borders.

“The change of government, the [Labour] manifesto pledge to reset the relationship and then particularly the May summit last year really did put us on a new trajectory,” says Uta Staiger, a researcher in European studies at University College London and an adviser on Brexit to the institution’s leadership.

But the country’s has not yet resumed its pre-Brexit role as a cornerstone of European research, and further UK–EU reset talks, slated for next month, have stalled. The agenda has not yet been published, but a major sticking point and focus of talks is thought to be the structure of the youth mobility scheme.

“Funding is recovering,” says Vassiliki Papatsiba, who studies research and policy at Cardiff University, UK. But it will be harder to re-build the United Kingdom’s credibility and networks of research collaboration, she says. “The progress is promising, but I do not think that the same position will be recovered in the foreseeable future.”

Funding uncertainty

UK–EU research relations are still recovering from the shock result of the Brexit referendum in June 2016, which had an immediate impact on research collaborations and applications to European funding programmes, even before the United Kingdom officially left the EU on 31 January 2020.

Many EU-based researchers were cautious about building funding proposals around UK partners when the country’s eventual relationship with the EU was unclear, says Papatsiba. “And uncertainty is risk.” Although replacement UK ‘guarantee’ government funding meant that researchers in the country could still take part in Horizon Europe calls, they could not lead collaborations.

Data published earlier this month by the UK government show that the share of EU funding for UK researchers fell from a peak of 16% in 2015 to 5.8% in 2023 (when including guarantee funding). Collaboration patterns also changed: papers co-authored by EU and UK researchers dropped from a peak of 60% of UK output in 2015 to 52% in 2022, according to an analysis published in November1 led by Yusuf Oldac, who studies research policy at the Education University of Hong Kong. There is some evidence that UK researchers might have adapted by seeking funding elsewhere, he says, with data showing that collaborations with researchers in East Asia grew markedly during the period.

The UK’s post-Brexit EU science deal: a graphical guide

But UK participation in EU research funding has begun to rebound since the country rejoined Horizon Europe — the share of EU grants awarded to UK scientists hit 9.3% in 2024. Although this is well below the pre-Brexit peak, data from the EU’s Horizon Dashboard (which is based on when agreements were signed, rather than calls announced) suggest that this upward trend will continue.

“Our time out in the desert has affected participation rates,” says Staiger. “It’s quite natural that it might take some time to bed back down.”

On the Horizon

Key to rebuilding European partnerships will be the United Kingdom’s role in the next iteration of Horizon Europe, from 2028 to 2034 — with a proposed budget of €175 billion, a hike of 50% in real terms. EU governments are still negotiating the programme’s form and final budget, and it is the first time the United Kingdom has no official voice or vote.

As an associate country, rather than a member, the United Kingdom’s ability to shape European research policy has diminished, says Dani Payne, who leads education-policy research at the Social Market Foundation, a think tank in London. The European Commission is also boosting funding for defence and dual-use research with both civilian and military applications. Collaborations with UK-based researchers could face more oversight, and the UK could be shut out of certain EU funding streams altogether because of regulations that allow participation only from EU countries.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a positive and significant development in the restoration of UK-EU scientific collaboration and funding after Brexit, highlighting the UK rejoining major EU research programs and the recovery of research funding shares. This has broad, meaningful impact on the scientific community and innovation across Europe, with detailed context on the progress and challenges ahead.
articles.personality_title: UK and EU rebuild scientific ties with new research funding and student exchanges
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context**  
After the United Kingdom voted to leave the European Union in 2016, its involvement in European scientific research and funding dropped sharply. This created challenges for UK researchers and their European partners. Over the past few years, efforts have been made to restore these collaborations and funding opportunities.

**What happened**  
In 2024, the UK rejoined Horizon Europe, a major EU research funding program worth €95 billion. Since then, the UK’s share of EU research funding has started to rise again, reaching 9.3% of grants awarded in 2024. Additionally, starting in 2027, the UK will rejoin Erasmus+, the EU’s exchange program that allows students and university staff to study and work across Europe. UK and EU leaders have also begun talks to further strengthen science cooperation.

**Impact**  
These developments mean UK scientists and students can again more easily collaborate with European partners and access important funding. This helps improve research quality and innovation. However, the UK still has less influence on EU science policy than before Brexit, and rebuilding trust and networks will take time. The return to EU programs also helps young people gain experience abroad, which benefits education and future careers.

**What's next step**  
Further discussions between the UK and EU are planned to finalize details of cooperation, especially around youth mobility programs like Erasmus+. The next phase of Horizon Europe, running from 2028 to 2034 with a bigger budget, will be a key test for UK-EU research ties. Although the UK won’t have voting rights in EU decisions, successful participation could strengthen scientific partnerships.

**One-sentence takeaway**  
The UK is gradually restoring its role in European science through renewed funding and student exchange programs, marking progress in post-Brexit collaboration.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Royaume-Uni et l’UE renouent leurs liens scientifiques avec de nouveaux financements et échanges étudiants
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte**  
Après le vote du Royaume-Uni en 2016 pour quitter l’Union européenne, sa participation à la recherche scientifique européenne et aux financements a fortement diminué. Cela a créé des difficultés pour les chercheurs britanniques et leurs partenaires européens. Ces dernières années, des efforts ont été faits pour restaurer ces collaborations et opportunités de financement.

**Ce qui s’est passé**  
En 2024, le Royaume-Uni a réintégré Horizon Europe, un important programme européen de financement de la recherche d’une valeur de 95 milliards d’euros. Depuis, la part du Royaume-Uni dans les financements européens a commencé à augmenter, atteignant 9,3 % des subventions en 2024. De plus, à partir de 2027, le Royaume-Uni rejoindra à nouveau Erasmus+, le programme d’échanges de l’UE qui permet aux étudiants et personnels universitaires d’étudier et de travailler en Europe. Des discussions ont aussi commencé entre dirigeants britanniques et européens pour renforcer la coopération scientifique.

**Impact**  
Ces avancées facilitent la collaboration des scientifiques et étudiants britanniques avec leurs partenaires européens et l’accès à des financements importants. Cela améliore la qualité de la recherche et l’innovation. Cependant, le Royaume-Uni a moins d’influence sur la politique scientifique européenne qu’avant le Brexit, et il faudra du temps pour reconstruire la confiance et les réseaux. Le retour aux programmes européens aide aussi les jeunes à acquérir une expérience à l’étranger, bénéfique pour leur éducation et leur avenir professionnel.

**Prochaine étape**  
D’autres discussions sont prévues pour finaliser les détails de la coopération, notamment autour des programmes de mobilité des jeunes comme Erasmus+. La prochaine phase d’Horizon Europe, de 2028 à 2034 avec un budget plus important, sera un test clé pour les liens scientifiques entre le Royaume-Uni et l’UE. Même sans droit de vote dans les décisions européennes, une participation réussie pourrait renforcer ces partenariats.

**Résumé en une phrase**  
Le Royaume-Uni restaure progressivement son rôle dans la science européenne grâce à des financements renouvelés et des programmes d’échanges étudiants, marquant un progrès dans la coopération post-Brexit.
articles.personality_title_es: Reino Unido y UE reconstruyen lazos científicos con nuevos fondos e intercambios estudiantiles
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto**  
Después de que el Reino Unido votara en 2016 salir de la Unión Europea, su participación en la investigación científica europea y en financiamiento cayó mucho. Esto creó dificultades para los investigadores británicos y sus socios europeos. En los últimos años, se han hecho esfuerzos para restaurar estas colaboraciones y oportunidades de financiamiento.

**Qué pasó**  
En 2024, el Reino Unido volvió a unirse a Horizon Europe, un importante programa de financiamiento europeo para investigación con un valor de 95 mil millones de euros. Desde entonces, la participación del Reino Unido en los fondos de la UE ha comenzado a subir, alcanzando un 9.3 % de las subvenciones otorgadas en 2024. Además, desde 2027, el Reino Unido volverá a participar en Erasmus+, el programa de intercambio de la UE que permite a estudiantes y personal universitario estudiar y trabajar en Europa. También comenzaron conversaciones entre líderes británicos y europeos para fortalecer la cooperación científica.

**Impacto**  
Estos avances facilitan que científicos y estudiantes británicos colaboren con socios europeos y accedan a financiamiento importante. Esto mejora la calidad de la investigación y la innovación. Sin embargo, el Reino Unido tiene menos influencia en la política científica europea que antes del Brexit, y reconstruir la confianza y las redes tomará tiempo. El regreso a los programas de la UE también ayuda a los jóvenes a ganar experiencia en el extranjero, beneficiando su educación y futuro profesional.

**Próximo paso**  
Se planean más conversaciones para finalizar detalles de la cooperación, especialmente sobre programas de movilidad juvenil como Erasmus+. La próxima fase de Horizon Europe, de 2028 a 2034 con un presupuesto mayor, será una prueba clave para los lazos científicos entre Reino Unido y UE. Aunque el Reino Unido no tendrá derecho a voto en decisiones europeas, una participación exitosa podría fortalecer las asociaciones científicas.

**Resumen en una frase**  
El Reino Unido está restaurando gradualmente su papel en la ciencia europea mediante la renovación de fondos y programas de intercambio estudiantil, marcando un avance en la colaboración post-Brexit.
articles.image_prompt: Two stylized hands, one adorned with the Union Jack pattern and the other with the European Union flag stars, gently reaching toward each other over a glowing open book symbolizing shared knowledge and research, set against a soft background of interconnected puzzle pieces in muted blues and reds, evoking collaboration and rebuilding bonds.

articles.title: SpaceX wants to build AI data centers in space. Will it work?
articles.summary: The race to build data centers in space is gaining momentum as AI drives unprecedented demand for computing power. Orbital facilities could tap into abundant solar energy and avoid many of the environmental challenges faced on Earth. Yet space remains a harsh and expensive place to operate, with major hurdles including cooling, maintenance, radiation exposure, and orbital debris.
articles.content:
Imagine if one company could become the railroad, electric utility and cloud-computing provider of the emerging space economy. That potential fueled excitement around the long-anticipated initial public offering of SpaceX. Investors are not simply betting on rockets anymore. They are betting on an entire orbital ecosystem.

Among the most ambitious and challenging ideas riding this wave of enthusiasm is something that sounds almost like science fiction: orbital data centers. SpaceX may be one of the most well-known companies seeking to build them, but it is not the only one.

The logic is seductive: Launch the data centers into orbit, where solar energy is abundant and land, water and local power grids are no longer constraints. As artificial intelligence drives an explosion in computing demand, companies are pitching orbital data centers as a way to escape the growing environmental and infrastructure pressures of Earth-based computing. Data centers often also face backlash from the public at having these centers located in their communities.

But there is a vast difference between launching satellites and operating an industrial-scale computing infrastructure in orbit. Space is unforgiving. Radiation damages electronics. The electronics generate enormous amounts of heat, and getting rid of that heat is surprisingly difficult in space. Repairs are extraordinarily expensive, and every pound launched into orbit still carries a significant cost.

We are engineering professors who study data center design and space systems engineering. Building a space-based data center will involve considerations from both sides.

What goes into a data center on Earth

First off, consider what goes into an Earth-based data center, like those that you’ve probably begun to see pop up everywhere. These facilities power cloud computing, video streaming, online banking, scientific computing and, increasingly, artificial intelligence. But a data center is much more than a room full of servers.

A data center needs several things to operate reliably. The first is electric power. Servers, networking equipment and storage devices consume large amounts of electricity, and that power demand is growing rapidly with AI.

The second is cooling. Almost all the electricity consumed by servers eventually becomes heat. If that heat is not removed quickly and reliably, equipment performance drops, failures increase and the data center can shut down. Cooling systems often include air handling units, chillers, cooling towers, pumps and, increasingly, liquid-cooling equipment. In many facilities, cooling is the largest energy consumer after the computing equipment itself.

The third is physical infrastructure, including the necessary land, buildings, structural support, backup power, water systems, communication networks and maintenance access. Data centers also need to be close enough to users and network backbones to provide fast digital services.

In short, Earth-based data centers are large electrical and thermal infrastructure systems built around computing hardware.

Placing them in space

So what would it take to build these data centers in space, and why are companies finding this possibility such an interesting business proposition?

As on Earth, these data centers would require massive amounts of power. In space, this power would come from solar panels. The Sun always shines in space and can’t be blocked by clouds. However, depending on the orbit the solar panels are put in, the Earth may shadow them for some portion of the orbit.

And even the best solar cells available today can convert only about half the sunlight that hits them to electricity.

Another potential advantage found in space is cooling. The cold background of space (near minus 455 degrees Fahrenheit, or minus 270 degrees Celsius) creates an opportunity: waste heat from the data center could escape into space through radiators, keeping the electronics cool.

In principle, that design could eliminate some of the bulky and water-intensive cooling infrastructure used on Earth. However, those thermal radiators would require a large amount of surface area, and that would be in addition to the area required by the solar panels.

In space, there is no air to blow across hot equipment and help heat escape. The heat has to leave as infrared radiation, which is a relatively slow process. As a result, removing 10 megawatts of waste heat can require radiator surfaces comparable to the size of two football fields.

Space-based data centers could also avoid some of the local conflicts that come with building large data centers on the ground. Many communities resist new data center developments because of their land use, energy and water demand, and noise and environmental impact.

A space-based system would avoid competing for local land and water resources, and it would not generate neighborhood noise or require local zoning approval in the same way.

However, space is already getting crowded, and launching thousands of large orbital data centers would accelerate this issue. Orbital debris and micrometeorites are hazards because they can puncture the space data center, and a worst-case collision could destroy it and create even more space debris.

The frequency of space launches necessary to send all the equipment to orbit may also become a concern for some communities. SpaceX has had protests at its launch complex in Boca Chica, Texas, from local activists who argue that its rocket testing and launches damage the surrounding environment.

All that data would need to be sent between Earth and these data centers – and between the data centers themselves – using radio waves or laser communications systems. Although satellite constellations such as Starlink and Amazon Leo have demonstrated that doing this is possible, the amount of data sent to and from space would balloon.

Additional challenges

These data centers, along with their solar panels and radiators, cannot be launched in one piece and would need to be assembled in space. This process would require new equipment for in-space servicing, assembly and manufacturing.

Another key challenge is the refresh cycle of computing hardware. Data center servers are not built to last forever. Operators on Earth usually replace or upgrade hardware every three to five years as chips improve, workloads change and equipment ages.

And equipment failures can require replacing components. The refresh and repair processes are relatively straightforward on Earth, where workers can physically remove and replace servers.

In space, refresh and repair becomes much harder. Hardware sent to orbit may be difficult or too expensive to upgrade. If the computing platform cannot be updated, or too many components fail, it may become obsolete long before the surrounding infrastructure reaches the end of its useful life.

In a field where performance improves so rapidly and demand from computing continues to increase, this hurdle could prove a major economic and operational challenge.

Then there is the harshness of space. These data centers would be in a near vacuum, with constant radiation hitting them. And depending on their orbit, they would go from hot when in the sunlight to cold in Earth’s shadow many times a day. All of these challenges, and more, are issues that will need to be addressed.

So, do they still make sense?

Despite these challenges, companies are moving forward with designing space-based data centers. SpaceX just announced the design for its AI1 Compute Satellite, which it hopes to use as an orbital data center spacecraft. However, this satellite is 100 to 1,000 times less capable than current Earth-based data centers.

Not every computing task makes sense to do in space. Many data center applications depend on fast response times and close connections to users on Earth. Financial transactions, interactive AI services and most cloud applications are extremely sensitive to delay.

More feasible early applications may be those that are less latency-sensitive and more tightly connected to space operations. Examples could include processing Earth observation data from satellites, military or intelligence data processing, scientific computing related to space missions, or specialized computing for satellites and other space assets.

In other words, the first viable space data centers may serve space-based customers before they compete with mainstream cloud data centers on Earth.
articles.reasoning: The article discusses the ambitious and innovative effort to build AI data centers in space, which could provide significant benefits such as abundant solar energy use, reduced environmental impact on Earth, and new infrastructure for the emerging space economy. Despite substantial technical challenges, the concept represents a meaningful technological breakthrough with broad implications for computing and space industries, potentially transforming data processing and energy use on a large scale.
articles.personality_title: SpaceX plans AI data centers in orbit to tap solar power and ease Earth’s limits
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – As artificial intelligence (AI) needs more and more computing power, companies are looking for new ways to build data centers. Data centers are places full of computers that store and process information. SpaceX and other companies want to build these centers in space, where solar energy is always available, and Earth’s land and water limits won’t be a problem.

**What happened** – SpaceX announced plans for its AI1 Compute Satellite, a small orbital data center. Unlike Earth-based data centers, these centers would use solar panels to get power and radiators to send heat out into space. But building and running data centers in space is difficult because of harsh conditions like radiation, extreme temperatures, and the cost of repairs. The centers must be launched in parts and assembled in space, and updating or fixing hardware will be much harder than on Earth.

**Impact** – Putting data centers in space could reduce the environmental impact on Earth by using clean solar power and avoiding land and water use. It could also help with some space activities that need a lot of computing, such as processing satellite data or helping space missions. However, because space is crowded and expensive, and because communication between Earth and space can be slow, these centers will likely serve special tasks rather than replacing Earth’s main data centers.

**What’s next step** – Companies will continue testing and designing space data centers, starting with smaller, less powerful satellites like SpaceX’s AI1. Researchers and engineers will work on solving problems like cooling, repairs, and data transfer. The first space data centers might support space-related projects before handling common internet services.

**One-sentence takeaway** – SpaceX and others aim to build AI data centers in orbit to use constant solar energy and reduce Earth’s resource limits, though many technical challenges remain before they can become widely useful.
articles.personality_title_fr: SpaceX prévoit des centres de données IA en orbite pour utiliser l’énergie solaire et alléger les limites terrestres
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Avec les besoins croissants en puissance informatique de l’intelligence artificielle (IA), des entreprises cherchent de nouvelles façons de construire des centres de données. Ces centres sont des lieux remplis d’ordinateurs qui stockent et traitent des informations. SpaceX et d’autres veulent construire ces centres dans l’espace, où l’énergie solaire est toujours disponible et où les limites de la terre et de l’eau n’existent pas.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – SpaceX a annoncé son projet AI1 Compute Satellite, un petit centre de données orbital. Contrairement aux centres terrestres, ces centres utiliseraient des panneaux solaires pour l’électricité et des radiateurs pour évacuer la chaleur dans l’espace. Mais construire et faire fonctionner des centres dans l’espace est difficile à cause des conditions extrêmes comme les radiations, les températures très variables et le coût des réparations. Les centres doivent être lancés en morceaux et assemblés dans l’espace, et mettre à jour ou réparer le matériel sera plus compliqué qu’au sol.

**Impact** – Installer des centres dans l’espace pourrait réduire l’impact environnemental sur Terre en utilisant l’énergie solaire propre et en évitant l’utilisation des terres et de l’eau. Cela pourrait aussi aider certaines activités spatiales qui ont besoin de beaucoup de calcul, comme traiter les données des satellites ou aider les missions spatiales. Cependant, comme l’espace est encombré et coûteux, et que la communication Terre-espace peut être lente, ces centres serviront probablement des tâches spécifiques plutôt que de remplacer les centres terrestres.

**Prochaine étape** – Les entreprises continueront à tester et concevoir des centres de données spatiaux, en commençant par des satellites plus petits et moins puissants comme l’AI1 de SpaceX. Les chercheurs travailleront à résoudre les problèmes de refroidissement, de réparations et de transfert de données. Les premiers centres dans l’espace pourraient soutenir des projets liés à l’espace avant de gérer des services internet courants.

**Message clé en une phrase** – SpaceX et d’autres veulent construire des centres de données IA en orbite pour profiter de l’énergie solaire constante et réduire les limites terrestres, même si de nombreux défis techniques restent à relever.
articles.personality_title_es: SpaceX planea centros de datos IA en órbita para aprovechar la energía solar y aliviar las limitaciones terrestres
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – A medida que la inteligencia artificial (IA) necesita cada vez más potencia informática, las empresas buscan nuevas formas de construir centros de datos. Estos centros son lugares llenos de computadoras que almacenan y procesan información. SpaceX y otras compañías quieren construir estos centros en el espacio, donde la energía solar está siempre disponible y no existen las limitaciones de tierra y agua que hay en la Tierra.

**Qué pasó** – SpaceX anunció su plan para el AI1 Compute Satellite, un pequeño centro de datos orbital. A diferencia de los centros en la Tierra, estos usarían paneles solares para obtener energía y radiadores para enviar el calor al espacio. Pero construir y operar centros de datos en el espacio es difícil por las condiciones duras como la radiación, las temperaturas extremas y el alto costo de las reparaciones. Los centros deben lanzarse en partes y ensamblarse en el espacio, y actualizar o reparar el hardware será mucho más complicado que en la Tierra.

**Impacto** – Poner centros de datos en el espacio podría reducir el impacto ambiental en la Tierra al usar energía solar limpia y evitar el uso de tierra y agua. También podría ayudar en actividades espaciales que necesitan mucha computación, como procesar datos de satélites o apoyar misiones espaciales. Sin embargo, debido a que el espacio está lleno y es caro, y la comunicación entre la Tierra y el espacio puede ser lenta, estos centros probablemente atenderán tareas especiales en lugar de reemplazar los centros principales en la Tierra.

**Próximo paso** – Las empresas seguirán probando y diseñando centros de datos espaciales, comenzando con satélites más pequeños y menos potentes como el AI1 de SpaceX. Investigadores y ingenieros trabajarán para resolver problemas de enfriamiento, reparaciones y transferencia de datos. Los primeros centros espaciales podrían apoyar proyectos relacionados con el espacio antes de manejar servicios comunes de internet.

**Conclusión en una frase** – SpaceX y otros buscan construir centros de datos IA en órbita para usar la energía solar constante y reducir las limitaciones terrestres, aunque aún quedan muchos desafíos técnicos por superar.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a futuristic, glowing orbital data center shaped like a sleek satellite adorned with expansive solar panels and large radiators, gently orbiting Earth against a deep space backdrop, with soft light reflecting off its surfaces and subtle beams of data represented as flowing, luminous threads connecting it to a distant, stylized Earth below.
